text: The Nathanson liver retractor is positioned to elevate the left lobe of the liver, providing clear exposure of the surgical field.
The surgeon prepares to use a 36 French gastric bougie during the sleeve gastrectomy procedure.

The surgeon deflates the abdomen and pushes it towards the lesser curvature. Dissection of the short gastric vessels begins, with the left hand using a Deaver atraumatic retractor and the right hand elevating the omentum. A harmonic scalpel is employed to achieve a proper plane of dissection.
The surgeon begins the slow separation of the greater omentum from the stomach, using careful dissection techniques to detach these structures.
The surgeon uses a suction device to aspirate fumes generated by the harmonic scalpel during the dissection process.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel, which vibrates at 55,000 vibrations per second, creating vapors during dissection. A suction device is employed to clear the vapors, ensuring a clear field of vision.
The spleen becomes visible as the surgeon continues the separation of the greater omentum from the stomach using robotic assistance.

The surgeon carefully separates posterior adhesions near the spleen using a harmonic scalpel, maintaining a safe distance to prevent bleeding. The tip of the harmonic scalpel is kept away from the spleen to avoid accidental injury.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to safely separate the posterior adhesions, maintaining a clear field of vision by aspirating fumes with suction.
The surgeon uses the harmonic scalpel to carefully dissect tissue up to the left crus, ensuring a clear field of vision while avoiding proximity to the spleen.
The surgeon examines the posterior wall for any remaining adhesions and proceeds to move towards the pylorus, ensuring the surgical field is clear.
The surgeon dissects through the antrum, ensuring a distance of six to eight centimeters is maintained from surrounding structures to prevent complications.
The surgeon lifts the stomach, revealing adhesions on the posterior wall that are visible as the tissue is elevated.
The surgeon safely separates the posterior wall adhesions while retracting the bougies to prevent inappropriate traction during dissection.
The surgeon completes the dissection, addressing a small area of adhesion, while the bougie is pushed down to maintain proper positioning.
The surgeon uses an instrument to negotiate the lotus near the lesser curvature of the stomach, ensuring proper direction. An upper GI endoscope may be utilized for enhanced visualization.

The surgeon observes an indentation on the stomach caused by the bougie, which is directed towards the pylorus and positioned near the lesser curvature. The stapling process is about to begin, with the choice of stapler being at the surgeon's discretion.
The surgeon selects between the Tri-Stapler or Ethicon Echelon, Echelon-Six-T stapling devices for use in the procedure.
The surgeon begins dissecting the sleeve of the stomach, maintaining proximity to the bougie, which is positioned to guide the dissection along the lesser curvature.
The surgeon addresses any bleeding encountered during the procedure by applying a titanium clip, similar to those used in cholecystectomy, to compress and stop the bleeding.
The surgeon uses suction to check for any bleeding in the surgical field, followed by the application of a stapler, which can be operated either robotically or manually by the assistant.
The surgeon examines the posterior valve and continues stapling to separate the stomach, using six or seven cartridges to create the sleeve.
The surgeon approaches the gastroesophageal junction, maintaining a safe distance from the esophagus to avoid creating 'dog ears,' which are potential sites for leaks due to reduced blood supply.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to safely separate any minor adhesions from the posterior wall of the stomach.
The surgeon inspects the stapler line for any bleeding and begins continuous suturing using a barb suture, such as V-lock, along the stapler line.
The surgeon takes the seromuscular layer of both the posterior and anterior walls of the stomach, burying the staple margin into the seromuscular layer to secure the closure.
The surgeon uses the suture to apply traction on the stomach, exposing the posterior wall for continued suturing along the staple line.

The surgeon carefully avoids picking vessels while taking a bite during suturing to prevent hematoma formation. A titanium clip is applied to control minor oozing from bleeders, followed by continued suturing.

The surgeon cuts the barb suture without needing a termination knot, as it holds on its own. The needle is removed, and the procedure transitions to the cholecystectomy part. The existing ports, initially placed for sleeve gastrectomy, are used. The patient has a visibly fatty and slightly enlarged left lobe of the liver, making the cholecystectomy ergonomically challenging due to the patient's obesity and reduced distance from the right lobe of the liver to the costal margins.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to manipulate and articulate around the gallbladder, despite limited retraction of the liver. Minimal adhesions are visible, and the surgeon begins separating these adhesions from the gallbladder.
The surgeon attempts to stay close to the gallbladder during dissection. The fundus traction is limited as the fundus clasp cannot be advanced towards the diaphragm.
The surgeon uses the left da Vinci robotic grasper to apply traction to the Hartmann's pouch of the gallbladder, facilitating exposure for further dissection.
The surgeon performs posterior peritoneal dissection while applying anterolateral traction to the gallbladder, facilitating the separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon identifies the cystic duct, which appears plain, and begins to separate the attached anterior peritoneum to achieve a critical view of safety.
The surgeon defines the Calot's triangle, revealing the cystic duct and artery after completing posterior peritoneal dissection.

The surgeon reveals both the posterior and anterior windows, with a visible lymph node aiding in the identification of the artery. Adhesions are separated, providing a clear view of Calot's triangle, where the cystic duct and artery are distinctly visible.
The surgeon prepares to clip the artery using a titanium medium large size clip, without the use of ICG for duct mapping. The harmonic device is used for hemostasis.

The surgeon separates the artery and identifies the duct, applying three titanium clips on the duct: two clips towards the common bile duct (CBD) and one towards the gallbladder. The clips are spaced with a three-millimeter gap between the first and second, and a six-millimeter gap between the second and third, ensuring safe cutting.

The surgeon uses scissors to cut between the second and third clips on the cystic duct, revealing the lumen clearly. The gallbladder is then separated from the liver, with bleeding checked before continuing further dissection and separation.

The surgeon applies anterolateral traction to define the areolar plane for dissection, gradually separating the gallbladder from the liver. Due to limited space between the liver and costal margins, the gallbladder is pulled inferiorly, with traction occasionally directed towards the fundus.

The surgeon completes the cholecystectomy, checking for any bleeding in the surgical field. A suction device is used briefly, followed by the insertion of an endo bag to remove the stomach and gallbladder together, confirming no visible bleeding.

The surgeon injects 50 ml of methylene blue to inspect the staple line. The gallbladder and the remaining stomach are visible, with the separated stomach nearby. The endo bag is opened wide to accommodate both the stomach and gallbladder for removal.

The surgeon uses a claw forceps to securely hold the neck of the gallbladder, preventing transverse arrest during extraction. The specimen, including the gallbladder and stomach, is retracted through a 15 mm or 12 mm port using an endo bag. After removal, the surgeon closes the ports under direct vision to prevent incision-related complications. Irrigation and suction are applied, followed by the injection of methylene blue using an endoscope.
An upper GI endoscope is introduced to inspect the margins of the staple line, and methylene blue is injected through the endoscope for visualization.
